text: We have shown that precise adjustment of the structure of metal-organic frameworks at the sub-angstrom level is highly effective as a means to increase gas selectivity, yet often little effect is noted for larger pore structures. In this study, an integrative design approach consisting of a sequence of ultraprecise structural adjustments (USAs) in MOFs has been successfully realized using the multi-modular partitioned acs (pacs) platform that can tolerate significant numbers of isoreticular substitutions and functional variations. Bioisosteric substitutions using 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate (BDC) with the small, cyclic dicarboxylate ligand, trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate (tcb) resulted in the increase of the c/a ratio in the unit cell by a remarkable 30% through the expansion of the μ4-O2TCF bridges, providing an "extremely compressed" pore structure. Further adjusting the structure of the resulting compressed MOF only by less than 3% via a second series of USAs using a smaller ligand led to an increase in C2H2/CO2 selectivity from 2.6 to 20.8 and confirmed further through the gas breakthrough studies. The use of small, cyclic dicarboxylates, such as tcb, thus opens an entirely new avenue for sub-angstrom structural adjustments in MOFs for highly selective gas separations.
explanation: The passage is dense with domain-specific MOF jargon and precise numerical results, typical of a scientific abstract; while this can be human-written, the polished, self-contained style also aligns with AI-generated content. The evidence slightly leans toward AI due to its compact, assumption-light presentation.
